beats.text: A dense layer flattens an image and wastes weights, so a convolution slides one small kernel across every location instead.
A 224 by 224 RGB picture becomes 150,528 inputs, and a single dense unit reading all of them owns that many weights.
The output at each position is the sum of element-wise products between the kernel and the patch it overlaps, plus a bias.
A dense 32 by 32 to 32 by 32 map needs about a million weights, while a 3 by 3 convolution needs only nine plus a bias.
With no padding the feature map shrinks: an N by N input convolved with a k by k kernel yields an N minus k plus one output.
Weight sharing forces position-independence, the prior that lets convolutional networks generalize from far less data.
Convolution is equivariant, but classification wants invariance, so pooling converts a little equivariance into a little invariance.

visual.sub: Summarize each window with one number

beats.text: Max-pooling slides a p by p window with stride s and reports only the strongest activation inside each region.

visual.tex: P(i,j) = \max_{0\le m<p,\;0\le n<p} S(i\cdot s + m,\; j\cdot s + n)

beats.text: A stride-2 pool halves each dimension, cutting the feature map to a quarter of its area and cheapening every layer above.
Because the max ignores where the peak occurred, a small shift that keeps it in the same window leaves the output unchanged.
Pooling can hurt tasks that need position, so many architectures since the mid-2010s downsample with strided convolutions instead.
A color photo is height by width by three channels, so each kernel takes the same depth and dots over all channels at once.
A layer's parameter count and output height follow directly from kernel size, channels, padding, and stride.

visual.note: Conv layer parameters and output geometry

beats.text: A 3 by 3 conv with 256 input and 256 output channels has about 590,000 parameters, independent of the image size.
As pooling shrinks the spatial grid, channel counts rise to compensate, trading where for what as the network goes deeper.
A typical backbone runs from 224 squared with 3 channels down to 7 squared with 512, small and deep before the classifier.
A 1 by 1 convolution does no spatial mixing, acting as a per-pixel linear layer that cheaply changes channel depth.
The CNN's history is a tight sequence of ideas, each one fixing the ceiling the previous generation ran into.

visual.sub: Each idea fixed the last one's ceiling

beats.text: Depth climbed from LeNet's 7 layers in 1998 to ResNet's 152 in 2015, the span that defines the modern backbone.
Through 2014 deeper meant better, yet past about twenty layers accuracy got worse from an optimization failure, not overfitting.
The residual block fixes it with one change: learn only the residual and add the original input back at the end.
The additive shortcut gives gradients a clean plus-one path back to early layers, enabling networks up to 152 layers deep.

visual.label: layers unlocked by the skip connection

beats.text: Two stacked 3 by 3 convs cover a 5 by 5 field with 18 weights instead of 25, so 3 by 3 became the field's default kernel.
The most practically important fact about CNNs is that the features they learn transfer to entirely new tasks.
A backbone trained on ImageNet's 1.2 million photos learns a near-universal vocabulary of edges, textures, and object parts.
Feature extraction freezes all convolutional weights and trains only a fresh classifier on top of the final feature vector.
Fine-tuning unfreezes part of the backbone at a learning rate 10 to 100 times lower, nudging the weights rather than erasing them.

visual.note: Small learning rate preserves pre-trained weights

beats.text: Feature transferability falls with depth, so early layers transfer almost perfectly while the last layers stay task-specific.
Match the preprocessing first, since mismatched resolution, normalization, or channel order is the most common silent failure.
